fidelity_problems: Attributes an unsubstantiated 'approximately 30%' improvement claim to the pre-mortem technique. Gary Klein's original work and his 2007 HBR article do not cite a specific 30% figure; this appears fabricated or pulled from an unrelated source, and an agent citing it would spread false precision.

The decision flow diagram implies binary confidence-checking ('Team confident? → yes → PRE-MORTEM ESSENTIAL') which misrepresents Klein's nuanced view. Klein advocated pre-mortems when teams are overconfident OR when uncertainty is high, not as a blanket gate on all confident teams.

The '20+ failure reasons collected' threshold in the verification checklist is invented—Klein's method emphasizes quality and diversity of perspectives, not an arbitrary volume quota. Forcing 20+ reasons encourages noise and dilutes signal.
mislead_scenario: An AI agent is asked to do a solo risk assessment for a small personal coding project. Following this skill literally, the agent would simulate a group pre-mortem alone—'gathering the team' (itself), conducting 'silent brainstorming' and 'round-robin sharing' with imaginary participants, and forcing 20+ failure reasons by inventing increasingly implausible scenarios. The output would be bloated, theatrical, and less useful than a simple checklist or structured risk matrix. The entire value of the pre-mortem comes from diverse human perspectives and the psychological safety of group legitimization of dissent—neither of which applies when an agent does this alone. The agent wastes tokens and produces worse analysis than a straightforward 'what could go wrong?' exercise.
missing_boundaries: Does not state that pre-mortems are a GROUP facilitation technique and lose most of their value when conducted solo by an individual (human or AI).
Does not warn against using this for already-decided decisions or for post-hoc justification of a choice already made (pre-mortems then become rationalization theater).
Does not address pre-mortem fatigue or overuse—running pre-mortems on every sprint, every decision, and every launch creates cynicism and dilutes the technique's impact.
No boundary against trivial or low-stakes work where the ceremony cost exceeds the risk-reduction benefit.

Fails to distinguish between genuine risk identification and anxiety-driven catastrophizing—an agent lacking emotional calibration may spiral into listing every conceivable failure mode no matter how improbable.

Does not mention that pre-mortems explicitly assume the project 'failed spectacularly' which can be psychologically inappropriate for contexts involving safety-critical systems, trauma-informed teams, or high-anxiety environments where catastrophic framing causes distress rather than insight.
weakest_part: The 'Why Pre-Mortems Work' section lists five psychological mechanisms (legitimizes pessimism, bypasses groupthink, uses hindsight bias, creates psychological safety, surfaces hidden knowledge) that ALL depend on group dynamics among humans in a room. An AI agent reading this skill—which is literally injected into its context as a reasoning tool—will apply these mechanisms as if it can replicate them internally, when it cannot. The section conflates 'why pre-mortems work for human teams' with 'why this is a useful thinking tool for an AI' without drawing that distinction, making the entire skill anthropomorphically misleading.
highest_leverage_fix: Add an explicit 'AI Agent Adaptation' section at the top that says: this skill describes the canonical human-team process; when used by an AI agent alone, the agent should skip the group facilitation mechanics (silent brainstorming, round-robin) and instead use the core reframe ('assume failure, work backward') as a structured prompt to systematically enumerate risks across the given categories, then prioritize and propose mitigations—without simulating imaginary team members or inventing 20+ reasons when 5-8 substantive ones suffice.
